coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection system based on dual-driving catalysis of CuO nanozyme with a classical univariate calibration for the determination of ascorbic acid in serum and fruits
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ke
Liu, Jian
Wang, Xin
Liu, Xiaoyang
Hu, Jinshuang
Li, Enshou
Zhao, Yusai
Zhao, Rusong
Yang, Shenghong
coredata.dc:description: Copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) as nanozymes have received extensive attention due to their numerous advantages such as water solubility and biomimetic effects. In this work, we combined the inherent oxidase and ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity of CuO NPs to construct a ratiometric fluorescence system for the detection of ascorbic acid (Vitamin C, AA) in serum and fruits. By feat of the dual mimic nanozymatic activity of CuO NPs, the sole substrate o-phenylenediamine (OPD) can be catalyzed to attain yellow fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD), while oxidized AA will react with the pristine OPD to form blue fluorescent product. This method has a sensitive response to AA with a good linear relationship in the concentration range of 1–150 μM and low detection limit of 0.16 μM. Practical sample detection of AA in human serum and fruits can be realized reasonably with the recovery rates between 84.83% and 87.28% and 88.53%-113.1%, respectively. More conveniently, the method can be successfully used for scanning measurement analysis through the associated application (ColorAssist) on smartphones and portable UV lamps.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CuO nanozyme
Ratiometric fluorescent
Ascorbic acid
Fruits

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ascorbic acid analysis by CuO-AA&OPD system
Dual enzyme catalysis effects of CuO nanoparticles
Detection of AA in real human serum and fruit samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21806189 ), Jiangxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 20202BABL214002 ), the Young Doctor Cooperative Foundation of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (No. 2019BSHZ0027 ), Taishan Scholar Project of Shandong Province (No.ts20190948) and Program for Scientific Research Innovation Team in Colleges and Universities of Shandong Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image and HRTEM image (insert) (a), size distribution (b), and XRD pattern (c) of synthesized CuO NPs, XPS survey scan for Cu 2p (d) and O 1 s (e), FTIR spectra of CuO NPs (f), UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CuO NPs (g).

The nanozyme catalytic effect of CuO NPs to AA (a) the fluorescent specturm of DFQ from the reaction of CuO-AA&OPD system (b); the oxidation effect of CuO NPs to OPD (c), the fluorescent spectrum of oxOPD from the reaction between OPD and CuO NPs (d).
Fluorescence (a) and UV–Visable spectra (b) of OPD, OPD-AA, OPD-CuO, and OPD-CuO-AA with excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Inset: corresponding fluorescent and optical photographs from left to right).

The Fluorescence spectra of the method in presence of different concentrations of AA (a), the plot of I435/I565 values versus the concentrations of AA (b), the selectivity of ratiometric fluorescence for AA detection toward l-arginine, l-glutamic acid, cysteine, l-serine, glycine, malic acid, glucose, melamine, thiourea, lactose, urea, cellulase, glucose oxidase, gastric protease, chymotrypsin (AA: 30 μM; cellulase, glucose oxidase, gastric protease and chymotrypsin: 10 U/mL; others: 30 μM) (c). Images of sample solutions containing various concentrations of AA under 365 UV lamp (d), the liner relationship between the ratio of (G + B)/R and AA concentrations (e), the selectivity of ratio of (G + B)/R and images of sample solutions for AA detection toward l-arginine, l-glutamic acid, cysteine, l-serine, glycine, malic acid, glucose, melamine, thiourea, lactose, urea, cellulase, glucose oxidase, gastric protease, chymotrypsin (AA: 100 μM; cellulase, glucose oxidase, gastric protease and chymotrypsin: 10 U/mL; others: 100 μM) (f).
The construction of ratiometric fluorescence driven by the double action of CuO NPs and its application in scanning analysis.
Comparison of different works for the sensing of AA.
Determination of AA in human serum and fruit samples.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection system based on dual-driving catalysis of CuO nanozyme with a classical univariate calibration for the determination of ascorbic acid in serum and fruits

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) as nanozymes have received extensive attention due to their numerous advantages such as water solubility and biomimetic effects. In this work, we combined the inherent oxidase and ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity of CuO NPs to construct a ratiometric fluorescence system for the detection of ascorbic acid (Vitamin C, AA) in serum and fruits. By feat of the dual mimic nanozymatic activity of CuO NPs, the sole substrate o-phenylenediamine (OPD) can be catalyzed to attain yellow fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD), while oxidized AA will react with the pristine OPD to form blue fluorescent product. This method has a sensitive response to AA with a good linear relationship in the concentration range of 1–150 μM and low detection limit of 0.16 μM. Practical sample detection of AA in human serum and fruits can be realized reasonably with the recovery rates between 84.83% and 87.28% and 88.53%-113.1%, respectively. More conveniently, the method can be successfully used for scanning measurement analysis through the associated application (ColorAssist) on smartphones and portable UV lamps.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C, AA), as an important water-soluble vitamin, is an essential nutrient for vital activities and plays an extremely important role in the daily lives of humans and animals [1,2]. As a free radical scavenger, it can not only participate in the biosynthesis of nerve mediators and hormones but also involve with the redox reaction of the body, maintaining the normal functions of teeth, bones, blood vessels and muscles [3,4]. The lack of AA can cause skin bruises, bleeding gums, muscle weakness, joint swelling and even cancer, hence, the level of AA in blood is an important indicator of pathological processes [5,6]. According to the report of the Food and Nutrition Committee of the Institute of Medicine of the National Academy of Sciences, the daily intake of AA for the human body is about 2000 mg [7], and the necessary AA intake from fruits and vegetables can effectively cope with some unpredictable diseases. On the contrary, excessive intake of AA will cause series of negative effects, including urinary system stones, taste degradation and diarrhea [8,9]. Therefore, the sensitive determination of AA in human serum and food samples can provide references for the pathological process and reasonable dailyintake of AA, respectively [10,11].

Recent years, AA determination in various biological and food substrates have attracted more and more attention, researchers have done a large quantity of work on the detection analytical technique, such as chromatography, electrochemistry, colorimetry, fluorometry, and so on [12–17]. Among them, the fluorescent technique stands out due to its low cost, rapidity and sensitivity, which has become one of the focuses of attention in this field [18,19]. However, most of fluorescent detection technique reported for AA are based on single fluorescence quenching or enhancement, which are highly susceptible to instrumentation and environment changes [20–22]. Fortunately, ratiometric fluorescent analytical technique can effectively avoid system errors to provide more accurate analysis results by comparing the relative intensity of two fluorescence emission peaks instead of the single emission intensity changes [23,24]. More importantly, the changes of the two fluorescent emission peaks also can cause obvious colour changes that can be intuitively observed by the naked eye [25–27]. Therefore, the development of new robust ratiometric fluorescent analytical technique for AA detection is of significant.

The ratiometric fluorescent analytical technique can effectively eliminate system interference and improve sensitivity to some extent, while it still subjects to the improvement of analysis specificity. The interferences from compounds with properties similar to AA in real samples are still hard to overcome, such as glutathione and vitamin E [28,29]. Encouragingly, the natural active enzymes with excellent catalytic efficiency and specificity are extremely suitable for introducing analytical systems to improve selectivity, but their applications still suffer from high cost and low stability [30]. Compared with natural enzymes, nanozymes can achieve low-cost, large-scale production, and long-term storage stability under non-physiological environments, which overcomes above defects and can be used as an effective substitute for natural enzymes [31]. Nanozymes have been widely used in many fields, such as biological analysis, medicine, sensing, and environment, etc due to the excellent stability and specificity [32–35]. Therein, water-dispersed copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) have been proven to exhibit unique ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity of catalyzing AA to form dehydrogenous ascorbic acid (DHAA) in the presence of oxygen [36], which provides new ideas for constructing ratiometric fluorescent detection technique of AA with high sensitivity, accuracy, and selectivity.

In this work, we rationally designed an effective ratiometric fluorescent detection system of AA based on the ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity and oxidability of CuO NPs. On the one hand, AA can be rapidly oxidized to DHAA catalyzed by CuO NPs, and DHAA will react with non-fluorescent o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to form blue fluorescent 3-(1, 2-dihydroxyethyl) furo [3,4-b] quinoxaline-1-one (DFQ) [37,38]. On the other hand, non-fluorescent OPD can be oxidized by dissolved O2 trigged by CuO NPs to form yellow fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD), during which AA involved can impede the formation of oxOPD because of the effective coupling of DHAA and OPD. Based on that, a CuO-AA&OPD system was successfully constructed for ratiometric fluorescent detection of AA. The designed system only emit yellow fluorescence with emission wavelength of 565 nm in the absence of AA, but a new blue fluorescence band of 435 nm emerging accompanied by decrease in yellow fluorescence after adding AA (Scheme 1 ). The developed AA analysis method possesses high sensitivity and selectivity, which was successfully applied for AA detection in real serum and fruits samples. We further fabricate a scanometric quantitative model based on the changes of fluorescence colour observed by naked eyes to realize the simple and fast judgment of AA content. Compared to other studies, the main advantages of the study is not only just the ingenious combined utilization of oxidase activity and ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity of CuO NPs, but also the simplicity and effectiveness of ratiometric signal construction based single OPD as sensing substrate, which may be provide valuable reference for the reasonable application of nanozymes in analysis field.

O-phenylenediamine (OPD) and copper acetate were purchased from Energy Chemical-Saen Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ascorbic acid (AA) and ethanol were obtained from Aladdin Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaOH was received from Tianjin Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glacial acetic acid was received from Kermel Chemical reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water with a resistance of 18.25 MΩ·cm was used throughout the whole work (UPR-II-40L, Sichuan, China).

The fluorescence properties were measured on a F97Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). A UV 2800S spectrophotometer (Shanghai Shunyuhengping technologies Co., LTD, China) was used to record the absorption spectra. The morphology information of the CuO was recorded by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30, USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) measurement was performed using a D8 ADVANCE03030502 X’Pert diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The particle size distribution of CuO was characterized by a Laser particle size analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Panalytical, Britain). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was obtained on X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALABXi + ). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were carried out on a Nexus 670 spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet, USA).

CuO NPs was prepared by a quick-deposition method according to ref. 38 with some modifications. Typically, 150 mL of copper acetate aqueous solution (0.02 M) was mixed with 0.5 mL of glacial acetic acid, and the mixture was heated to boil under vigorous stirring. Then, 10 mL of sodium hydroxide solution (0.04 g·mL−1) was quickly added to the boiling mixture, and a large amount of black precipitate formed immediately. The black precipitate was obtained through centrifugal treatment (8000 r·min−1) and purified by ethanol washing three times. Finally, the black products CuO NPs were dried in a vacuum oven at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ascorbic acid analysis by CuO-AA&OPD system
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, a CuO-AA&OPD system was constructed to realize the ratiometric fluorescent determination of AA. 240 μL solution (10 mM OPD), 20 μL CuO NPs (pH = 6.85) stock solution with a concentration of 38.25 μg·mL−1, and different concentrations of AA were mixed with 3 mL citric acid-disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer solution (5 mM, pH = 7.0). The buffer solution of 5 mM can keep the pH of sensing system stable and not affected by exogenous AA and CuO NPs. The mixed solution was incubated in a water bath at 45 °C for 40 min, and the fluorescent intensities at 435 nm and 565 nm of CuO-AA&OPD sensing system were measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The intensity ratios of 435 nm to 565 nm (I435/I565) under different concentrations AA (pH = 4.37) added were calculated to draw standard curve. Series of compounds were selected evaluated the selectivity of designed sensing system, including l-arginine, l-glutamic acid, cysteine, l-serine, glycine, malic acid, glucose, melamine, thiourea, lactose, urea, cellulase, glucose oxidase, gastric protease, chymotrypsin, and the process is the same as above.

Vitamin C injection with a original concentration of 50 mg·mL−1 was purchased from local pharmacy as non-spiked standard samples. Human serum samples were collected from two healthy adult males at the Qilu University of Technology Hospital. For the determination of AA in serum, the human blood sample was centrifuged first, and the upper layer of plasma was further purified through ultrafiltration to removing higher molecular weight organics (centrifugal speed is 4500 r·min−1). The serum sample obtained after the pretreatment is then diluted and measured. Various fresh fruits, including lemon, watermelon, cherry, grape, cherry tomatoes, were purchased from local supermarkets. The fruits were borke into juice, then filtered and diluted for further use. Standard addition experiments also carried out to verify the accuracy of proposed method.

Series of techniques such as TEM, DLS, XRD, and XPS, etc. were used to characterize the as-synthesized CuO NPs. As shown in Fig. 1 a, CuO NPs exhibit an uniformly spherical morphology, which is consistent well with the previous report [39]. HRTEM image proved that the particle size of CuO NPs is about 10 nm (Fig. 1a inset). DLS results suggest that the size distribution of CuO NPs is between 7 and 13 nm with a average size of about 9.56 nm (Fig. 1b). XRD analysis was used to verify the purity and monoclinic structure of the CuO NPs. The synthesized CuO NPs have no obvious impurity peaks, and the characteristic diffraction peaks of CuO NPs are consistent with JCPDS No.72–0629 (Fig. 1c) [40]. Fig. S1 showed the XPS survey result of CuO NPs. In Fig. 1d, the main peaks of Cu 2p3/2 at 933.8 eV and Cu 2p1/2 at 953.8 eV confirm the presence of Cu2+ chemical state [41,42]. In addition, weak satellite peaks at 941.2 eV, 943.7 eV, and 962.1 eV also correspond to Cu2+ species. In Fig. 1e, typical peak at 533.0 eV is attributed to CuO, proving the successful synthesis of CuO NPs, while the peak at 531.4 eV is assigned to surface adsorbed oxygen [41]. FTIR result suggests that the peaks at 519.2 cm−1, 1404.4 cm−1, and 3390.7 cm−1 are assigned to Cu–O, C-O, and –OH stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. 1f) [43]. In addition, the typical absorption peak of CuO NPs can be obersrved at 285 nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1g) [44]. All above results indicate the successful preparation of CuO NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual enzyme catalysis effects of CuO nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: In order to construct reasonable ratiometric fluorescent detection system of AA, dual enzyme catalysis effects of CuO NPs were verified carefully. Chuanuwatanakul et al. have revealed that AA can be catalyzed to produce DHAA in the presence of O2 trigged by the inherent ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity, and DHAA can react with non-fluorescent OPD to further form blue fluorescence DFQ (Fig. 2 a) [45]. From Fig. 2b we can see the obvious fluorescence signal of DFQ at 435 nm obtained from AA-CuO-OPD. UV–Vis absorption spectrum was carried out to further characterize the catalysis process. In Fig. S2, AA has a significant absorption peak at 265 nm, while the absorption peak of DHAA is weak. The AA can be catalyzed to generate DHAA trigged by CuO NPs proved by the sharply reduced characteristic peak at 265 nm (Fig. S3). Under the same conditions, the CuO NPs leachate cannot cause significant changes in absorption peaks, which demonstrates that the catalytic action comes from CuO NPs rather than possible Cu2+ in the leachate (Fig. S4). Furthermore, CuO NPs also have sufficient oxidase mimetic activity to catalyze the oxidation of OPD to form yellow fluorescence oxOPD (Em = 565 nm) in the presene of O2 (Fig. 2c-d). As shown in Fig.S5, under same conditions, the CuO NPs leachate cannot induce OPD to produce fluorescent oxOPD, which indicate the oxidability mainly derives by CuO NPs rather than Cu2+ that may leak from the CuO NPs. In order to further prove the enzyme activity and stability of CuO NPs, Cu2+ is directly acting on OPD and AA. As shown in Fig. S6-7, Cu2+ at the approximate to the concentration of CuO can only play weak effect on AA and OPD, which are far from CuO NPs. All above results provide a strong support for high specificity ratiometric fluorescent determination of AA.

Driven by the dual nanozyme properties of CuO NPs, a ratiometric fluorescence sensing system for AA detection was cleverly constructed. As shown in Fig. 3 a, OPD and the mixture of OPD and AA are non-fluorescent, while the OPD-CuO can emit obvious yellow fluorescence based on catalytic oxidation. In presence of AA, the conversion of OPD to oxOPD decreased because of the reaction between OPD and DHAA. Hence, with the increasement of AA, the yellow fluorescence of oxOPD at 565 nm decreased and the blue fluorescence band of DFQ at 435 nm increased meanwhile, for which the designed ratiometric fluorescence change was described as I435/I565. The absorption peak of OPD will decrease after the OPD is condensed with DHAA (Fig. S8). The addition of AA not only consumed OPD but also shared the competitive catalytic active sites on CuO NPs to retard the generation of oxOPD. Thus, the UV absorption peaks at 285 nm and 420 nm were reduced as the addition of AA (Fig. 3b).

The catalytic conditions are optimized to achieve the satisfactory effect. For AA converted to DHAA, as shown in Fig. S9a-b, the optimal pH and temperature is 7–9 and above 45 ℃, respectively. Next, as shown in Fig. S9c, pH 7 is the optimal oxidation condition for the catalytic oxidation of OPD, and the oxidation rate of the OPD is enhanced with the increase of temperature (Fig. S9d). In order to ensure the best dual catalytic effect, the pH = 7 and 45 ℃ were selected. Furthermore, the effects of substrate and catalyst concentrations on the ratio signal also were investigate systemically. The ratiometric fluorescence achieves the best response with the concentration of OPD and CuO NPs at 0.8 mM and 0.4 μg·mL−1, respectively (Fig. S10a-b). Besides, from Fig. S10c, the signal plateaued when incubation time exceeds 40 min. Furthermore, zeta potential was used to character the stability of CuO NPs in sensing system. From Table S1 we can see that the zeta potentials of CuO NPs can keep almost unchanged after OPD or AA involved, which prove that the CuO NPs possess well colloidal stability in proposed sensing system.

In addition, the specificity of analysis method is a key factor to ensure the accuracy of the measurement results. In this study, the inherent dual mimetic nanozyme activities of CuO NPs make the designed system possesses higher selectivity. Based on that, as shown in Fig. 4c, only AA can cause obvious ratio change while commonly bioactive molecules cannot interfere the sensing system, including reduced l-arginine, l-glutamic acid, cysteine, l-serine, glycine, malic acid, glucose, melamine, thiourea, lactose, urea, cellulase, glucose oxidase, gastric protease, chymotrypsin. We can see all proposed active molecules except AA cannot give rise to the fluorescence colour change, which indicate that the constructed method has good selectivity.

The scanometric analysis, as an emerging quantitative method, exhibits the advantages of simple, fast, low cost, and without the aid of large-scale instrument, which is extremely suitable for the on-site analysis and quick sample deterimination [58–61]. Therefore, we further develop a scanometric quantitative model based on the fluorescence colour change for the fast detection of AA. As shown in Fig. 4d-e, it can be clearly observed that the fluorescent color gradually changes from yellow to blue with the increase of AA concentrations. The RGB values of images were readout by a APP (ColorAssist developed by FTL apps) on the Smartphone, and the ratio value of the sum of G and B to R ((G + B)/R) was calculated to digitize the colour change induced by different AA concentrations. A rough linear curve can be obtained within the range of 1–150 μM (R2 = 0.9954), and the detection limit is about 0.30 μM based on 3 times the signal-to-noise ratio, which is sufficient for practical application. Similarly, the selectivity of scanometric analysis is also verified by scanning images. As shown in Fig. 4f, we can see all proposed active molecules except AA cannot give rise to the fluorescence colour change, and only AA can cause significant changes in the value of (G + B)/R, indicating that the method of scanometric analysis also has good selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in real human serum and fruit samples
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the accuracy of constructed method was evaluated through the Vitamin C injection for medical purpose as non-spiked standard sample. From Table S2 we can see that the levels of AA in diluted Vitamin C injection can be detected presisely with satisfactory roverises of 98.3%-103.2%. To explore the feasibility and practicability of designed ratiometric fluorescence analysis method, the content of AA in human serum and various fruits were detected as representative of practical biological and food samples, respectively. For human serum, the sample was preprocessed firstly according standard. The results are shown in Table 2 , the detected AA in human serum is about 26.2 μM, and the initial content of AA in fresh lemons, watermelons, cherries, grapes, cherry tomatoes was 73.43, 11.36, 73.55, 17.56 and 13.93 μM, respectively. The spiked experimental results show that the recoveries are between 84.83% and 113.1%, and the RSDs are no more than 4.3%. Statistical test was applied to further check the accuracy of proposed method [46]. At 95% confidence level, as detailed in Table 2, the method is accurate since texp < t(0.05,11). All above results suggest that the designed method can be applied for the effective detection of AA in real samples.

In summary, we have fabricated a high-efficiency ratiometric fluorescence method to detect AA. This method is constructed by using CuO NPs to promote the only signal precursor by means of the inherent dual mimetic nanozyme activities. Ratiometric fluorescence method was established from the changes between the yellow and blue fluorescence. The developed method has a sensitive response to AA with the detection limit of 0.16 μM. With low cost and simple operation, the ratiometric fluorescence established can complete the detection of actual samples in human serum and various fruits and has the potential use in real life. More importantly, this method can realize rapid on-site detection through portable UV lamps without requiring large instruments, which greatly improves the practical value of this method.

Ke Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Jian Liu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Xin Wang: Validation. Xiaoyang Liu: Methodology. Jinshuang Hu: Data curation. Enshou Li: Resources. Yusai Zhao: Visualization. Rusong Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Shenghong Yang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21806189), Jiangxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No.20202BABL214002), the Young Doctor Cooperative Foundation of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (No.2019BSHZ0027), Taishan Scholar Project of Shandong Province (No.ts20190948) and Program for Scientific Research Innovation Team in Colleges and Universities of Shandong Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106921.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ self-assembled cationic lanthanide metal organic framework membrane sensor for effective MnO4 - and ascorbic acid detection
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.C. Sun, S. Hao, R.Q. Fan, J. Zhang, W. Chen, K. Zhu, P. Wang, X.K. Fang, Y.L. Yang, In situ self-assembled cationic lanthanide metal organic framework membrane sensor for effective MnO4- and ascorbic acid detection, Anal. Chim. Acta 1142 (2021) 211-220.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as fluorescent/colorimetric probes for real-time detection of hypochlorite and ascorbic acid in cells and body fluid
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.N. Wei, H.Q. Li, S.B. Liu, W. Wang, H.L. Chen, L.H. Xiao, C.L. Ren, X.G. Chen, Carbon dots as fluorescent/colorimetric probes for real-time detection of hypochlorite and ascorbic acid in cells and body fluid, Anal. Chem. 91 (2019) 15477-15483.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots derived from flax straw for highly sensitive and selective detections of cobalt, chromium, and ascorbic acid
J. Colloid Interf. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.K. Hu, L. Ge, Y.Y. Li, M. Mukhtar, B. Shen, D.S. Yang, J.G. Li, Carbon dots derived from flax straw for highly sensitive and selective detections of cobalt, chromium, and ascorbic acid, J. Colloid Interf. Sci. 579 (2020) 96-108.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An electrochemical sensor based on the modification of platinum nanoparticles and ZIF-8 membrane for the detection of ascorbic acid
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ma, Y.L. Zhang, L.S. Wang, An electrochemical sensor based on the modification of platinum nanoparticles and ZIF-8 membrane for the detection of ascorbic acid, Talanta 226 (2021) 122105.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Using neural networks to estimate the losses of ascorbic acid, total phenols, flavonoid, and antioxidant activity in asparagus during thermal treatments
J. Agric. Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.F. Lu, H. Zheng, H.Q. Lou, L.L. Jiang, Y. Chen, S.S. Fang, Using neural networks to estimate the losses of ascorbic acid, total phenols, flavonoid, and antioxidant activity in asparagus during thermal treatments, J. Agric. Food Chem. 58 (2010) 2995-3001.
